hypotheses: H1: Bimodal (polarized) rating distributions are perceived as more self-expressive than unimodal or non-polarized distributions.

H2: Products with bimodal distributions for self-expressive attributes (e.g., style) are perceived as more self-expressive than bimodal distributions for less self-expressive attributes (e.g., quality).

H3: Individuals with greater need for self-expression — particularly those with low self-concept clarity (dispositional or situationally induced) — prefer products with polarized (bimodal) rating distributions.
H4: Preference for polarized products is stronger when individuals are motivated to express individual (vs. group) identity.
claim.hypotheses_location: Stated across the Introduction and operationalized/tested across Studies 1–5 (as described in the paper's study descriptions and hypotheses sections).
claim.statement: Products with polarized (bimodal) rating distributions are perceived as more self-expressive and become more desirable, especially for individuals low in self-concept clarity and in contexts where self-expression is important.
claim.statement_location: Abstract/Introduction and summarized across the study descriptions and general discussion of the paper.

data.source: Multiple behavioral experiments with human participants (recruited from online panels such as Amazon Mechanical Turk for at least one study); online experimental stimuli depicting manipulated rating distributions.
data.wave_or_subset: Not applicable (multiple independent experimental studies; specific studies referenced individually).
data.sample_size: Varies by study. Examples reported: Study reported N = 425 for the 2x2x2 shoes experiment; Study 1 analyses reference samples implied by F(1,200) and F(1,199) (approximately 200 per comparison); Study-specific sample sizes vary (e.g., t(106) implies ~108 participants in Study 5).
data.notes: Studies include multiple independent experiments manipulating rating distributions (bimodal vs. unimodal or other high-variance shapes), rated attributes (style vs. quality), and contexts (self-expressive vs. utilitarian). Measures include desirability indices (α reported for some studies), willingness-to-pay (WTP), perceived self-expressiveness; mediation analyses used (PROCESS bootstrapping). Participants recruited from online panel(s) (e.g., Amazon Mechanical Turk stated for one study).

method.description: Series of controlled experiments in which participants viewed manipulated product rating distributions (bimodal vs. unimodal or other non-polarized distributions) and evaluated products on perceived self-expressiveness, desirability, and willingness to pay. Studies manipulated or measured individual differences in self-concept clarity (measured dispositional or experimentally induced conflicted state), consumption context (self-expressive vs. utilitarian), and rated attribute (style vs. quality). Statistical analyses included ANOVA/linear models testing main effects and interactions, t-tests for group comparisons, and mediation analyses (PROCESS bootstrapping) to assess perceived self-expressiveness as a mediator.
steps: Design visualizations of rating distributions to represent bimodal (polarized) and unimodal/non-polarized conditions.
Recruit participants (e.g., via Amazon Mechanical Turk for at least one study).
Randomly assign participants to experimental conditions (rating distribution, context, rated attribute, identity condition) or measure individual differences (self-concept clarity).
Present product stimuli (e.g., movies, outfits, shoes, sweatshirts) with the manipulated rating distributions and attribute framing (style vs. quality).
Collect dependent measures: perceived self-expressiveness, desirability index (multi-item scale), willingness-to-pay, and other related scales (distinctiveness, informativeness).

Conduct statistical analyses: ANOVA and linear regression models to test main effects and interactions; t-tests for pairwise contrasts; mediation analysis using PROCESS bootstrapping to test perceived self-expressiveness as mediator.
Interpret interactions (e.g., distribution × attribute × context) and report effect sizes, test statistics, and confidence intervals.
models: Linear regression models with interaction terms (reporting b, SE, t values)
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes: perceived self-expressiveness, product desirability (desirability index), and willingness-to-pay (WTP).
independent_variables: Self-concept clarity (measured dispositional or experimentally manipulated conflicted state)
method.tools_software: Not specified explicitly; mediation analyses reported using the PROCESS bootstrapping method (commonly associated with Hayes' PROCESS macro), statistical tests (ANOVA, regressions, t-tests) reported but software (e.g., SPSS, R, or Python) not stated.

results.summary: Across eight experiments, bimodal (polarized) rating distributions were perceived as more self-expressive than unimodal or non-polarized high-variance distributions. Participants with low self-concept clarity (dispositional or experimentally induced) showed greater preference for bimodal products and higher WTP. Preference for bimodal products was stronger in self-expressive consumption contexts and when the polarization concerned self-expressive attributes (style) rather than utilitarian attributes (quality). Individual identity expression motives amplified preference for polarized products relative to group identity motives. Perceived self-expressiveness mediated the effect of bimodal distributions on desirability and WTP in self-expressive contexts.

numerical_results.direction: Bimodal perceived as more self-expressive than multimodal

Low self-concept clarity participants found bimodal products more desirable (negative b indicates direction as reported in paper)

Trend: conflicted individuals favored bimodal ratings (marginal)
Conflicted participants willing to pay more for bimodal-rated items
Higher desirability for bimodal outfits in gallery (self-expressive) context
Trend toward higher desirability for bimodal ratings when tied to self-expressive attribute in self-expressive context
